item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exploiting SCPs for Fun and Profit!
Episode 1876: "Paradox Xerox"

1) Have robot place SCP-1876 on table next to 1 kg bar of gold (or other portable object you'd like more of).
2) Pick up SCP-1876.
3) Immediately! pick up gold bar.
4) ???
5) You now have two bars of gold.

Use this helpful lifehack to fund your shadow-government organization or quickly restock when you find out there's only one beer left in the cooler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm quite impressed with this! It's a very simple, but subtle weird object. The only way they could tell just what the damn thing does is through the testing, but it's done in a very clever and believable way. I like this little thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cross-SCP testing aside, the testing can be expanded with other irreversible-change actions, like jumping into a pool, or out of a plane or pinching the First Lady on the buttock.

What would happen to a D-Class who would have the SCP sticky-taped to him via drone? Would it consider that a triggering action or not? Would it affect any action he takes after that or not? It got INTO that bin somehow, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I like this idea!

While I'm here… if the agent's gun hasn't been fired (so the sedatives are in there), and there's sedatives in the D-class's blood stream, that means the sedatives were copied :-o

I can see this being used to replicate things… put something valuable in your mouth (or swallow it, if that's what it takes), move the toothbrush, get "rewound", pick up the valuable thing and put it in your mouth, move the toothbrush, get "rewound"… the best part is that it doesn't even take any real world time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know not all items need a connection to a larger universe, but I want to believe this belonged to Eric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: LSD? Why not SCP-500?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be interesting to see some other tests of what's brought back in time (and duplicated) with the person. If sedatives in someone's bloodstream are brought back, what about other drugs? (SCP-500?) What if the drugs are held in parts of the person's body where they aren't dissolved, or in places where they are but they're prevented from dissolving? (Like how people smuggle drugs by putting them in balloons and swallowing them.) What if someone picks up the toothbrush and is able to change clothes before being sent back in time? What would they be wearing when they get back?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty simple SCP. The Description was written well enough to keep me interested, and the test logs were just amazing.

This is a great example of how to write an excellent SCP on a reasonably simple idea. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice little item, neat effect, and well-explored. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can think of a profit motive in this for both MC&D if it only injects chemicals in Class D's, and the Foundation if it rewinds killing the shit out of them.

(for the former, shoot the victim with absolutely insane amounts of LSD, extract from their blood upon rewind, rinse, repeat)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How on earth did I miss this up until now? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is a clever use of retrocausality.

A+. and a +1 for all of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spent the whole article thinking "But what if it just implants memories to make you think that's what happened? How would the Foundation tell the difference?". Then that objection was clearly and concisely dealt with in the last test log.

Elegantly done, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of weird shit going on at [REDACTED] University.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really hope we're not in a "harness the power of passive-aggressiveness" pattern.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last addendum was the one that showed this toothbrush's ability to mess up with time and space. Nicely done, simple and effective. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the message. The typo is pretty neutral, but the idea that some college kid might have made an SCP that prevented his roommate from taking his toothbrush really adds to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes.
This SCP.
Yes.
I wish there was a +5 button.
Now if only the Doctor had a temporal toothbrush.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, although now I wonder how it got thrown away in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why everyone's fixating on the tothbrush thing. The idea of someone using time travel to keep people from taken their stuff is hilarious, and the logs show a very logical progression of testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must be honest, I actually like this better than the Tothbrush. Its really well done and a very cool concept. I love the hint of mystery this toothbrush has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it affects it either way, and it makes sense given the context.

Also:"Subject reaches for SCP-1876 and pauses for several seconds while exhibiting surprise that rapidly changes to shock and then fear before breaking down into sobbing while pleading not to be shot." Is a pretty huge run on sentence. May want to edit it. (I don't know how to do quote blocks on my phone *sobs*).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not a great tothbrush. The test logs saved it from a downvote, though. You may want to reconsider how this is presented, as the effects really aren't apparent until you hit that point (I had a major "aha!" moment, and then I liked everything).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reaction in the second test is a little over the top for me, but the third one is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be perfectly honest, the typo was a last-minute, spur of the moment thing and really has nothing to do with the rest of the SCP except as one of those "strange, inexplicable details" I tend to throw in. Would the article be better without it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very clever, I like it. It differentiates itself from the Tothbrush well enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was just going to say "hey look, another typo-toothbrush".

This is pretty basic, but charming and decently executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of the World's Best Tothbrush, but I actually kind of like that reference (assuming it was intentional). I like the article quite a bit.
